Fern Polrrrcer- PRACTTcBs CoMMrssroN
428 J Street . Suite 620 o Sacramenro, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 r Fax (916) 322-0886
September 15,2006
Michael J. Shirey
Deputy City Attomey
City of Chula Vista
2T6FotrthAvenue
Chula Vista, CA 91910
RE: Your Request for Advice
Our FiIe No. A.-06-132
Dear Mr. Shirey:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Chula Vista City
Planning Commissioner Pamela Bensoussan regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions
of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").r Please note, the Commission will not advise
with respect to past conduct. (Regulation 18329(bX8XA) ) Therefore, nothing in this
letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct thatmay have aheady taken place, and
any conclusions contained in this letter apply only to prospective actions.
QUESTTONS
1. May Planning Commissioner Bensoussan participate in decisions to approve
an Urban Core Specific Plan and certify an Environmental Impact Report for the-Urban
Ctrre Specific Plan if she owns property located within 312 feetof the plan's study area?
2. If Commissioner Bensoussan may not participate in the foregoing decisions,
may she participate in public hearings on the Environmental Impact Report ind the
Urban Core Specific Plan as a member of the general public, after recusing herselfl
CONCLUSIONS
1. Commissioner Bensoussan has a disqualifying conflict of interest with respect
to decisions to approve the Urban Core Specific Plan and to certify the related
I Government Code sections 81000 * gl}14. Commission regulations appear at Titte 2, sections
18109-18997, of the Califomia Code of Regulations.
-@tiEl


File No. A-06-132
Page No. 2
Environmental Impact Report because she owns real property located within 500 feet of
the boundary of the areathat is the subject of the governmental decision.
2. Yes. If she recuses herself from voting, the Commissioner may participate in
public hearings as a member of the general public provided, however, that she limits her
comments to her personal interests, taking care to claify that she is not appearing in any
official capacity, and follows all of the procedures required by regulati on 18702.5
described below.
FACTS
The following facts were provided in your letter and in telephone conversations of
August 30 and September 5,2006.
The Community Development Department of the City of Chula Vista (the "City')
has prepared an Urban core specific Plan (the "Specific plan") that provides a
neighborhood-level plan for the city's urban core as identified in the city's General plan.
The Specific Plan addresses, atthe neighborhood level, land use mix and distribution,
zoning, urban and architectural design, vehicular and pedestrian circulation, parking,
transit services and facilities, public improvements, and implementation procedures. The
Specific Plan Study Area (the "study Area') covers approximately 1,700 acres within the
northwestern portion of the City. While there are 1,700 acres within the Study Area,
changes will focus on certain areas more in need of redevelopment. Therefore, the
Specific Plan focuses new zoningregulations and design guidelines for approximately
690 acres within the larger Study Area, denoted as the Specific Plan Sub Districts Area
(the "Sub Districts Area"). Existing zoningoutside of the Sub Districts Area is not
modified by the Specific Plan, and future development outside of the Sub Districts Area
will be processed under the existingzoningordinance.
There are223,423 residents and approximately 73,442 dwelling units in the City
with approximately 14,443 dwelling units in the Study Area. There are 50,101 property
owners in the City and 5,253 property owners in the Study Area.
Planning Commissioner Bensoussan owns property within 312 feet of the Study
Area and 655 feet from the Sub Districts Area. One of the properties is her primary
residence, which also includes a home office for a business she owns. The other is a
rental house that is rented to an individual.
Over the next several months, City staff intends to take the Specific Plan and the
related Environmental Impact Report ("EIR") prepared for the Specific Plan before the
plaruring commission for approval and certification, respectively. After consideration by
the Planning Commission, the EIR and the Specific Plan will go before the city council
for {inal action.


File No. A-06-132
Page No. 3
It is anticipated that the Specific Plan will have no effect on traffic or noise on the
affected areas, that the size of the affected parcels are about the same and that the overall
financial effect on homeowners will be to enhance their property values by about the
same percentage. You indicate that the Specific Plan was the subject of another Advice
Letter issued in2003, Hull AdviceLetter, No. 4-03-232.
The City of Chula Vista is a charter city governed by a council, consisting of four
council members and amayor, elected from the city at-large. The planning commission
is composed of seven members of the community appointed by thecity council from the
qualified electors of the City. City ordinance provides that actions by the planning
commission must be by the affirmative vote of a majority of the entire membership.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 of the Act provides that "no public official at any level of state or
local government shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his
official position to influence a govefirment decision in which he Lnows or has reason to
know he has afrnancial interest." Regulations 18700 through 18709 set forth an eight-
step analysis to determine whether a public official has a conflict of interest in making a
governmental decision.
step one. rs commissioner Bensoussan a o.public official, within the
meaning of section 87100?
Section 82048 defines a public official as "every member, officer, employee or
consultant of a state or local government agency." As a member of the planning
commission for the city of chula vista, which is a local government agency,
Commissioner Bensoussan is a public official. Therefore, she may not make, participate
in making, or otherwise use her position to influenc e any decisions that will have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any of her economic interests.
Step Two. As a member of the Planning Commission, wiII Commissioner
Bensoussan be making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental
decision?
A public official "makes a govemmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contracfual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulati on 18702.1.) A public official
"pafiicipates in a governmental decision" when, acting within the auihority of his or her
position and without significant substantive or intervening review, the official negotiates,
advises, or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker regarding the governmental
decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to use his or her official
position to influence a govemmental decision if, for the purpose of influencing the
decision, the official contacts or appears before or otherwise attempts to influenc e, atTy


File No. A-06-132
Page No. 4
member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.3.)
Moreover, the Commission has consistently advised that adoption of a specific plan,
including a decision relatingto design elements of the project, is a governmental decision
under the Act. (Busby Advice Letter, No. 4-05-159.) As a member of the Planning
Commission, Commissioner Bensoussan will be called upon to make or participate in
making decisions regarding the EIR and the Specific plan.
Regulation18702.5 requires that if apublic official who holds an office specified
in section 87200 has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, he or
she must immediately prior to the discussion of the item, orally identify on the record of
the meeting each t1.pe of economic interest involved in the decision, as well as details of
the economic interest, and recuse himself or herself from voting and leave the room for
the duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item. The Commissioner would still be
prohibited from voting on the decisions and from privately discussing these matters with
other members of the planning commission, city council or other city officials.
There is a narrow exception to the rule requiring the public official to leave the
rooms and which allows her to speak as any other member of the public to advocate on
behalf of her own personal interests. Under this exception, comments must be limited to
the Commissioner's own personal interests and she should take care to clarifir that she is
not appeaing in any official capacity. If Commissioner Bensoussan wishes to avail
herself of this exception, she must follow the exact procedures set forth in regulation
18702.s(d)(3).
Step Three. What are the Commissionerrs economic interests?
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts of interest arising
from certain enumerated economic interests. Section 87103 provides that apublic
official has a "financial interest" in a govemmental decision "if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have amateial financial effect, distinguishable from its
effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her immediate family,,'
or on any of the official's economic interests, described as follows:
A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he
or she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more. (Section
871 03(a); regulation I 8703. I (a).)
a
A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or
she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of
management. (Section 87 1 03(d); regulation 1 8703. 1 (b).)
A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she
has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b);
regulation 18703.2.)
a


a
a
a
File No. A-06-132
Page No. 5
An official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, totaling $500 or more within l2 months prior to the
decision. (Section 87103(c); regulation 18703.3.)
A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her
if the gifts total $360 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section
87 1 03(e); regulation 187 03.4.)
A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses,
income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family.
This is commonly referred to as the "personal financial effects" rule. (section
87 103 ; regulation 1 8703.5.)
Real Property - Presumably, Commissioner Bensoussan has at least a $2,000
economic interest in her single-family home. Accordingly, she has an economic interest
in this real property. The Commissioner also has an economic interest in her rental
property, assuming her ownership interest is worth $2,000 or more.
Busines=s Entities - Additionally, she would have an economic interest in the
business entitf renting out the p.op".ty and the appraisal business she operates out of her
home if she has an investment in them of $2,000 or more.
Sources of Income - The Commissioner also has an economic interest in her
tenant and the clients of her appraisal business as sources of income to her if she receives
$500 or more from them duringthe 72-month period prior to a governmental decision.
Step Four. Wiil the Commissioner's economic interests be directly or
indirectly involved in decisions she will make, participate in making or influence as
a planning commissioner?
Regulation 18704.1provides that"aperson, including business entities, sources
of income, and sources of gifts, is directly involved in a decision before an official's
agency when that person, either directly or by an agent: (1) Initiates the proceeding in
which the decision will be made by filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar
request; or (2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of the proceeding concerning the
decision before the official or the official's agency." Under your facts, there is nothing to
indicate that Commissioner Bensoussan's businesses or her sources of income will be
initiating proceedings or be the subject of proceedings regarding the EIR or the Specific
Plan. Accordingly, her rental and appraisal businesses and her sources of income would
be indirectly involved.3
2 "Business entity" means any organization or enterprise operated for profit, including a
proprietorship. (Section 82005.)


File No. A-06-132
Page No. 6
Regulation 18704.2(a) sets forth one of the circumstances under which real
property (including rental property) in which a public official has an economic interest is
deemed to be directly involved in a governmental decision:
"(1) The real property in which the official has an interest,
or any pafi of that real property, is located within 500 feet
of the boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the
property which is the subject of the goveflrmental decision.
For purposes of subdivision (a)(5), real property is located
'within 500 feet of the boundaries (or proposed boundaries)
of the real property which is the subject of the governmental
decision' if any part of the real property is within 500 feet
of the boundaries (or proposed boundaries) of the
redevelopment proj ect area."
The two governmental decisions you have inquired about are: (i) adoption of the
Specific Plan and (ii) certification of the EIR. Both decisions encompass the entire
Urban Core Specific Plan Area. The Commissioner's two properties are located within
500 feet of the Plan Area. Thus, both properties are directly involved in these decisions.
Step Five. What is the applicable materiality standard?
Economic Interest in Real Propertlr
The materiality standards applicable to the Commissioner's economic interest in
her real property are set forth in regulation 18705.2. The materiality standard applicable
to real property that is directly involved in a governmental decision is governed by
subdivision (aXl), which provides that "the financial effect of a governmental decision
on the real property is presumed to be material." This presumption may be rebutted by
proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the govemmental decision willhave any
financial effect, not even a penny, on the real property. (Regulation 18705.2(a)(1).) This
is commonly referred to as the "one penny ruIe," and that presumptionmay only be
rebutted if it is not reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will have any
financial effect, not even a penny's worth, upon the official's real property.
Economic Interest in Business Entities
The Commissioner's economic interest in her rental business and the business she
operates out of her home is governed by regulation 18705.1(c)(a) applicable to small
3 Business entities and sources of income that are not directly involved in governmental decisions
under the rules quoted above are regarded as indirectly involved. (Regulation 1 8704.1(b).)


File No. A-06-132
Page No. 7
businesses that are indirectly involved in governmental decisions.o It provides that the
financial effect of a governmental decision is mateial if it is reasonabiy foreseeable that
it will result in: (A) an increase/decrease in the business entity's fiscal year gross
revenues of $20,000 or more; (B) the business entity incurnnglavoiding additional
expenses or reducing/eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount $5,000
or more; or (C) an increase/decrease in the value of the business entity's assets/liabilities
of $20,000 or more.
Economic Interest in Sources of Income
The materiality standards applicable to sources of income vary depending on
whether the source of income is an individual or a business entity. In one of our
telephone conversations, you indicated that the Commissioner's tenant is an individual.
Regulation 18705.3(b)(3) sets forth the applicable standard for sources of income that are
indirectly involved in the govemmental decision. It provides that the effect of a decision
is material to an individual if the decision will affect the individual's income,
investments, or other assets or liabilities (other than real property) by $ 1,000 or more or
the decision will affect the individual's real property interest in a manner that is
considered matenal under regulation I S7 0 5 .2(b).
Step Six. Is it reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect of a
governmental decision on the Commissioner's economic interests will meet the
applicable materiality standard?
An effect is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if the effect is "substantially
likely." (Regulation 18706; In re Thorner (1975) 1 Fppc ops. 19g.) whether the
financial consequences of a govemmental decision are substantially likely at the time the
decision is made depends on the specific facts surrounding the decision. lfUiA.y a
financial effect need not be a certainty to be considered reasonably foreseeable. On the
other hand, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable. Each
goveflrmental decision coming before Commissioner Bensoussan where she will be
making, participating in making or influencing the decision must be examined
individually to determine if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
mateial financial effect on her economic interests.
Step Seven. Despite a determination that the Commissioner has a potential
conflict of interest, mflY she participate in a decision because the financial effect on
her interest is not distinguishable from its effect on the public generally?
A public official who determines that his or her economic interest will experience
amateial financial effect as a result of a governmental decision may nevertheless
participate in the decision if the financial effect on his or her interest is not
a Because you have not provided financial information regarding Commissioner Bensoussan,s
business interests, we assume the thresholds stated under subsection (c)(4) ofregulation 18705.1 apply.


File No. A-06-132
Page No. 8
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally. (Section 87I03,regulation
1,8707.) Under the basic rule provided in regulation 1 8707 .1, if a"significant segment,
of the jurisdiction is affected by the governmental decision in substantially the same
manner as it would affect the official's economic interests, then the official may
participate in the decision. Accordingly, in order to apply the "public generally,,
exception, an official must meet both parts of this two-part test-the decision must affect
a significant segment of the jurisdiction and the significant segment must be affected in
substantially the same manner as the official's interest.
Real Property - Significant Segment
Regulation 18707.1(b)(1XB) provides the standard used when the decision affects
an official's real property, where the significant segment is defined as (i) l0% or more of
all property owners or all homeowners in the jurisdiction of the official's agency or the
district the official represents; or (ii) 5,000 property owners or homeowners in the
jurisdiction of the official's agency.
You advised in our telephone conversation that there are 50,101 property owners
in the jurisdiction, and that there are 5,253 property owners within the Study Area-all
of whom will be affected by the decisions to approve the Specific Plan and the related
EIR. You have identified a significant segment of the public under regulation
18707(bxl)(B) with respect to the governmental decisions in question. You have not
provided facts indicating that this segment is affected by the govemmental decisions in
substantially the same manner as the public official. Your only indication as to what
impact the decisions would have was that the decisions would have a positive impact on
property values. We have no information as to the dollar value financial effect that the
government decisions at issue will have upon Commissioner's Bensoussan's real
property, let alone the effect on others in the segment. We have previously advised that
"what becomes critical for each official is identification of at least ten percent of the
homeowners within that segment that are impacted in substantially the same manner.,,
(Berger Advice Letter No. A-05-054.) In other words, while you state that other
property owners in the City will be affected by the governmental decisions, you provide
no facts to establish that the overall financial effect on the public official is substantially
similar to the other property owners in the significant segment.
We stated in the Myers Advice Letter No. 4-06-l0l,"frnancial effects are
measured in terms of values and not percentages."S In Berger (supra) we advised that
5 The letter goes on to state: "For example let us assume the reasonably foreseeable financial
effects of the governmental decision results in a five-percent increase in property values for the city as a
whole. The financial effect on a $1,000,000 properly would be twice as much as the financial effect on a
$500,000 property, and would not be considered substantially the same, even though the percent effect was
the same. Conversely, a financial effect of sii-percent on a $500,000 properfy rvo.rld b" substantially the
same as a three-percent increase on a $1,000,000 properfy, even though the percent is not the same.,,


File No. A-06-132
Page No. 9
the financial effect on properties within the "significant segment" could varyby a
specified financial runge equal to plus or minus two-percent of the value of the public
official's property and still be considered to be affected in "substantially the same
manner" as the official's property. (Emphasis added.)
Moreover, given the fact that there are two properties owned by the
Commissioner, it is unlikely that the dollar value impact on these two properties will be
substantially the same as on property owners' homes where they only own one home.6
Business Entities and Sources of Income - Sigrificant segrrents
Your facts address the "public generally" exception solely with respect to
govefiImental decisions affecting interests in real property. Should you provide
additional facts that would persuade us that the impact on the Comrnissioner's real
property will be substantially the same as on other property owners, she must still
consider her other economic interests. For example, if it is reasonably foreseeable that a
governmental decision will have amateial financial effect on her business entities or her
sources of income, she will need to perform the same kind of analysis to determine if
decisions affecting these economic interests fall within the "public generally" exception.
Step Eieht. If Commissioner Bensoussan has a conflict of interest in a
decision, may she nevertheless participate because her participation is IegaIIy
required?
Section 87101 (regulation 18708) provides an exception under which a public
official who has a conflict of interest in a governmental decision may nevertheless
participate. There is no suggestion in the facts you have provided that Commissioner
Bensoussan's participation in any planning commission decision, including decisions
involving the EIR or the Specific Plan, will be legally required.
6 You noted that the Commission had previously issued an advice letter regarding the same
Specific Plan and the "public generally" exception. The Hull Advice Letter No. A
-03-232, stated that "it appears that the'public generally' exception will permit Councilmember
Salas and Rindone to participate in the decision..." but that there were insufficienf facts ',to determine if
this exception applies to Councilmember McCann's situation." Like Commissioner Bensoussan,
Councilmember McCann had an economic interest in rental property. The letter was based on the premise
that an official must first measure the financial effects before making that determination, stating: ,;IJ th"r,
fficials determine that other homeowners located in the Study Area will be financially affecteJ byihe
decision in a manner substantially similar to the financial effect on their real property, the ,public
generally' exception would apply." (Emphasis added.) Hence, our conclusiorr h"ri ur" .r& inconsistent
with those inthe Hull advice letter.


File No. A-06-132
Page No. 10
If you have other questions on this matter,please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
By: Valentina Joyce
Counsel, Legal Division

may she participate in public hearings on the Environmental Impact Report ind the
Urban Core Specific Plan as a member of the general public, after recusing herselfl

1. Commissioner Bensoussan has a disqualifying conflict of interest with respect
to decisions to approve the Urban Core Specific Plan and to certify the related
-@tiEl
Environmental Impact Report because she owns real property located within 500 feet of
the boundary of the areathat is the subject of the governmental decision.
2. Yes. If she recuses herself from voting, the Commissioner may participate in
public hearings as a member of the general public provided, however, that she limits her

official capacity, and follows all of the procedures required by regulati on 18702.5
described below.

sections.analysis:

step one. rs commissioner Bensoussan a o.public official, within the
meaning of section 87100?
Section 82048 defines a public official as "every member, officer, employee or
consultant of a state or local government agency." As a member of the planning
commission for the city of chula vista, which is a local government agency,
Commissioner Bensoussan is a public official. Therefore, she may not make, participate
in making, or otherwise use her position to influenc e any decisions that will have a

Bensoussan be making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental
decision?

position to influence a govemmental decision if, for the purpose of influencing the
decision, the official contacts or appears before or otherwise attempts to influenc e, atTy
member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.3.)
Moreover, the Commission has consistently advised that adoption of a specific plan,
including a decision relatingto design elements of the project, is a governmental decision
under the Act. (Busby Advice Letter, No. 4-05-159.) As a member of the Planning
Commission, Commissioner Bensoussan will be called upon to make or participate in
making decisions regarding the EIR and the Specific plan.

in section 87200 has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, he or
she must immediately prior to the discussion of the item, orally identify on the record of
the meeting each t1.pe of economic interest involved in the decision, as well as details of
the economic interest, and recuse himself or herself from voting and leave the room for

or she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more. (Section

she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of
management. (Section 87 1 03(d); regulation 1 8703. 1 (b).)
A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she
has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b);
regulation 18703.2.)

promised income, totaling $500 or more within l2 months prior to the
decision. (Section 87103(c); regulation 18703.3.)

if the gifts total $360 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section
87 1 03(e); regulation 187 03.4.)
A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses,
income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family.
This is commonly referred to as the "personal financial effects" rule. (section
87 103 ; regulation 1 8703.5.)
Real Property - Presumably, Commissioner Bensoussan has at least a $2,000
economic interest in her single-family home. Accordingly, she has an economic interest
in this real property. The Commissioner also has an economic interest in her rental
property, assuming her ownership interest is worth $2,000 or more.
Busines=s Entities - Additionally, she would have an economic interest in the
business entitf renting out the p.op".ty and the appraisal business she operates out of her
home if she has an investment in them of $2,000 or more.
Sources of Income - The Commissioner also has an economic interest in her
tenant and the clients of her appraisal business as sources of income to her if she receives
$500 or more from them duringthe 72-month period prior to a governmental decision.
Step Four. Wiil the Commissioner's economic interests be directly or
indirectly involved in decisions she will make, participate in making or influence as
a planning commissioner?
Regulation 18704.1provides that"aperson, including business entities, sources
of income, and sources of gifts, is directly involved in a decision before an official's
agency when that person, either directly or by an agent: (1) Initiates the proceeding in
which the decision will be made by filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar
request; or (2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of the proceeding concerning the
decision before the official or the official's agency." Under your facts, there is nothing to
indicate that Commissioner Bensoussan's businesses or her sources of income will be
initiating proceedings or be the subject of proceedings regarding the EIR or the Specific
Plan. Accordingly, her rental and appraisal businesses and her sources of income would
be indirectly involved.3
2 "Business entity" means any organization or enterprise operated for profit, including a
proprietorship. (Section 82005.)
Regulation 18704.2(a) sets forth one of the circumstances under which real
property (including rental property) in which a public official has an economic interest is
deemed to be directly involved in a governmental decision:
"(1) The real property in which the official has an interest,
or any pafi of that real property, is located within 500 feet
of the boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the
property which is the subject of the goveflrmental decision.
For purposes of subdivision (a)(5), real property is located
'within 500 feet of the boundaries (or proposed boundaries)
of the real property which is the subject of the governmental
decision' if any part of the real property is within 500 feet
of the boundaries (or proposed boundaries) of the
redevelopment proj ect area."
The two governmental decisions you have inquired about are: (i) adoption of the
Specific Plan and (ii) certification of the EIR. Both decisions encompass the entire
Urban Core Specific Plan Area. The Commissioner's two properties are located within
500 feet of the Plan Area. Thus, both properties are directly involved in these decisions.
Step Five. What is the applicable materiality standard?
Economic Interest in Real Propertlr
The materiality standards applicable to the Commissioner's economic interest in
her real property are set forth in regulation 18705.2. The materiality standard applicable
to real property that is directly involved in a governmental decision is governed by
subdivision (aXl), which provides that "the financial effect of a governmental decision
on the real property is presumed to be material." This presumption may be rebutted by
proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the govemmental decision willhave any
financial effect, not even a penny, on the real property. (Regulation 18705.2(a)(1).) This
is commonly referred to as the "one penny ruIe," and that presumptionmay only be
rebutted if it is not reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will have any
financial effect, not even a penny's worth, upon the official's real property.
Economic Interest in Business Entities
The Commissioner's economic interest in her rental business and the business she
operates out of her home is governed by regulation 18705.1(c)(a) applicable to small
3 Business entities and sources of income that are not directly involved in governmental decisions
under the rules quoted above are regarded as indirectly involved. (Regulation 1 8704.1(b).)
businesses that are indirectly involved in governmental decisions.o It provides that the
financial effect of a governmental decision is mateial if it is reasonabiy foreseeable that
it will result in: (A) an increase/decrease in the business entity's fiscal year gross
revenues of $20,000 or more; (B) the business entity incurnnglavoiding additional
expenses or reducing/eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount $5,000
or more; or (C) an increase/decrease in the value of the business entity's assets/liabilities
of $20,000 or more.
Economic Interest in Sources of Income
The materiality standards applicable to sources of income vary depending on
whether the source of income is an individual or a business entity. In one of our
telephone conversations, you indicated that the Commissioner's tenant is an individual.
Regulation 18705.3(b)(3) sets forth the applicable standard for sources of income that are
indirectly involved in the govemmental decision. It provides that the effect of a decision
is material to an individual if the decision will affect the individual's income,
investments, or other assets or liabilities (other than real property) by $ 1,000 or more or
the decision will affect the individual's real property interest in a manner that is
considered matenal under regulation I S7 0 5 .2(b).
Step Six. Is it reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect of a
governmental decision on the Commissioner's economic interests will meet the
applicable materiality standard?
An effect is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if the effect is "substantially
likely." (Regulation 18706; In re Thorner (1975) 1 Fppc ops. 19g.) whether the
financial consequences of a govemmental decision are substantially likely at the time the
decision is made depends on the specific facts surrounding the decision. lfUiA.y a
financial effect need not be a certainty to be considered reasonably foreseeable. On the
other hand, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable. Each
goveflrmental decision coming before Commissioner Bensoussan where she will be
making, participating in making or influencing the decision must be examined
individually to determine if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
mateial financial effect on her economic interests.
Step Seven. Despite a determination that the Commissioner has a potential
conflict of interest, mflY she participate in a decision because the financial effect on
her interest is not distinguishable from its effect on the public generally?
A public official who determines that his or her economic interest will experience
amateial financial effect as a result of a governmental decision may nevertheless
participate in the decision if the financial effect on his or her interest is not
a Because you have not provided financial information regarding Commissioner Bensoussan,s
business interests, we assume the thresholds stated under subsection (c)(4) ofregulation 18705.1 apply.
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally. (Section 87I03,regulation
1,8707.) Under the basic rule provided in regulation 1 8707 .1, if a"significant segment,
of the jurisdiction is affected by the governmental decision in substantially the same
manner as it would affect the official's economic interests, then the official may
participate in the decision. Accordingly, in order to apply the "public generally,,
exception, an official must meet both parts of this two-part test-the decision must affect
a significant segment of the jurisdiction and the significant segment must be affected in
substantially the same manner as the official's interest.
Real Property - Significant Segment
Regulation 18707.1(b)(1XB) provides the standard used when the decision affects
an official's real property, where the significant segment is defined as (i) l0% or more of
all property owners or all homeowners in the jurisdiction of the official's agency or the
district the official represents; or (ii) 5,000 property owners or homeowners in the
jurisdiction of the official's agency.
You advised in our telephone conversation that there are 50,101 property owners
in the jurisdiction, and that there are 5,253 property owners within the Study Area-all
of whom will be affected by the decisions to approve the Specific Plan and the related
EIR. You have identified a significant segment of the public under regulation
18707(bxl)(B) with respect to the governmental decisions in question. You have not
provided facts indicating that this segment is affected by the govemmental decisions in
substantially the same manner as the public official. Your only indication as to what
impact the decisions would have was that the decisions would have a positive impact on
property values. We have no information as to the dollar value financial effect that the
government decisions at issue will have upon Commissioner's Bensoussan's real
property, let alone the effect on others in the segment. We have previously advised that
"what becomes critical for each official is identification of at least ten percent of the
homeowners within that segment that are impacted in substantially the same manner.,,
(Berger Advice Letter No. A-05-054.) In other words, while you state that other
property owners in the City will be affected by the governmental decisions, you provide
no facts to establish that the overall financial effect on the public official is substantially
similar to the other property owners in the significant segment.
We stated in the Myers Advice Letter No. 4-06-l0l,"frnancial effects are
measured in terms of values and not percentages."S In Berger (supra) we advised that
5 The letter goes on to state: "For example let us assume the reasonably foreseeable financial
effects of the governmental decision results in a five-percent increase in property values for the city as a
whole. The financial effect on a $1,000,000 properly would be twice as much as the financial effect on a
$500,000 property, and would not be considered substantially the same, even though the percent effect was
the same. Conversely, a financial effect of sii-percent on a $500,000 properfy rvo.rld b" substantially the
same as a three-percent increase on a $1,000,000 properfy, even though the percent is not the same.,,
the financial effect on properties within the "significant segment" could varyby a
specified financial runge equal to plus or minus two-percent of the value of the public
official's property and still be considered to be affected in "substantially the same
manner" as the official's property. (Emphasis added.)
Moreover, given the fact that there are two properties owned by the
Commissioner, it is unlikely that the dollar value impact on these two properties will be
substantially the same as on property owners' homes where they only own one home.6
Business Entities and Sources of Income - Sigrificant segrrents
Your facts address the "public generally" exception solely with respect to
govefiImental decisions affecting interests in real property. Should you provide
additional facts that would persuade us that the impact on the Comrnissioner's real
property will be substantially the same as on other property owners, she must still
consider her other economic interests. For example, if it is reasonably foreseeable that a
governmental decision will have amateial financial effect on her business entities or her
sources of income, she will need to perform the same kind of analysis to determine if
decisions affecting these economic interests fall within the "public generally" exception.
Step Eieht. If Commissioner Bensoussan has a conflict of interest in a
decision, may she nevertheless participate because her participation is IegaIIy
required?
Section 87101 (regulation 18708) provides an exception under which a public
official who has a conflict of interest in a governmental decision may nevertheless
participate. There is no suggestion in the facts you have provided that Commissioner
Bensoussan's participation in any planning commission decision, including decisions
involving the EIR or the Specific Plan, will be legally required.
6 You noted that the Commission had previously issued an advice letter regarding the same
Specific Plan and the "public generally" exception. The Hull Advice Letter No. A
-03-232, stated that "it appears that the'public generally' exception will permit Councilmember
Salas and Rindone to participate in the decision..." but that there were insufficienf facts ',to determine if
this exception applies to Councilmember McCann's situation." Like Commissioner Bensoussan,
Councilmember McCann had an economic interest in rental property. The letter was based on the premise
that an official must first measure the financial effects before making that determination, stating: ,;IJ th"r,
fficials determine that other homeowners located in the Study Area will be financially affecteJ byihe
decision in a manner substantially similar to the financial effect on their real property, the ,public
generally' exception would apply." (Emphasis added.) Hence, our conclusiorr h"ri ur" .r& inconsistent
with those inthe Hull advice letter.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. May Planning Commissioner Bensoussan participate in decisions to approve
an Urban Core Specific Plan and certify an Environmental Impact Report for the-Urban
Ctrre Specific Plan if she owns property located within 312 feetof the plan's study area?
2. If Commissioner Bensoussan may not participate in the foregoing decisions,
may she participate in public hearings on the Environmental Impact Report ind the
Urban Core Specific Plan as a member of the general public, after recusing herselfl

CONCLUSION: 1. Commissioner Bensoussan has a disqualifying conflict of interest with respect
to decisions to approve the Urban Core Specific Plan and to certify the related
-@tiEl
Environmental Impact Report because she owns real property located within 500 feet of
the boundary of the areathat is the subject of the governmental decision.
2. Yes. If she recuses herself from voting, the Commissioner may participate in
public hearings as a member of the general public provided, however, that she limits her
comments to her personal interests, taking care to claify that she is not appearing in any
official capacity, and follows all of the procedures required by regulati on 18702.5
described below.

Shirey Deputy City Attomey City of Chula Vista 2T6FotrthAvenue Chula Vista, CA 91910 RE: Your Request for Advice Our FiIe No. A.-06-132 Dear Mr. Shirey: This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Chula Vista City Planning Commissioner Pamela Bensoussan regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").r Please note, the Commission will not advise with respect to past conduct. (Regulation 18329(bX8XA) ) Therefore, nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct thatmay have aheady taken place, and any conclusions contained in this letter apply only to prospective actions. QUESTTONS 1. May Planning Commissioner Bensoussan participate in decisions to approve an Urban Core Specific Plan and certify an Environmental Impact Report for the-Urban Ctrre Specific Plan if she owns property located within 312 feetof the plan's study area? 2. If Commissioner Bensoussan may not participate in the foregoing decisions, may she participate in public hearings on the Environmental Impact Report ind the Urban Core Specific Plan as a member of the general public, after recusing herselfl CONCLUSIONS 1. Commissioner Bensoussan has a disqualifying conflict of interest with respect to decisions to approve the Urban Core Specific Plan and to certify the related I Government Code sections 81000 * gl}14. Commission regulations appear at Titte 2, sections 18109-18997, of the Califomia Code of Regulations. -@tiEl File No. A-06-132 Page No. 2 Environmental Impact Report because she owns real property located within 500 feet of the boundary of the areathat is the subject of the governmental decision. 2. Yes. If she recuses herself from voting, the Commissioner may participate in public hearings as a member of the general public provided, however, that she limits her comments to her personal interests, taking care to claify that she is not appearing in any official capacity, and follows all of the procedures required by regulati on 18702.5 described below. FACTS The following facts were provided in your letter and in telephone conversations of August 30 and September 5,2006. The Community Development Department of the City of Chula Vista (the "City') has prepared an Urban core specific Plan (the "Specific plan") that provides a neighborhood-level plan for the city's urban core as identified in the city's General plan. The Specific Plan addresses, atthe neighborhood level, land use mix and distribution, zoning, urban and architectural design, vehicular and pedestrian circulation, parking, transit services and facilities, public improvements, and implementation procedures. The Specific Plan Study Area (the "study Area') covers approximately 1,700 acres within the northwestern portion of the City. While there are 1,700 acres within the Study Area, changes will focus on certain areas more in need of redevelopment. Therefore, the Specific Plan focuses new zoningregulations and design guidelines for approximately 690 acres within the larger Study Area, denoted as the Specific Plan Sub Districts Area (the "Sub Districts Area"). Existing zoningoutside
